__comment_overview: FILE: monitoring/grafana/provisioning/dashboards/word-puzzle.json
PURPOSE:
This is a Grafana dashboard definition written in JSON.
Think of it as a 'blueprint' for a wall of charts.
Grafana reads this file (via dashboards.yml) and renders it as a
real interactive dashboard in your browser.
You do NOT need to understand every field here.
The important ones are explained with comments in the 'panels' array.
HOW IT WORKS (the full pipeline):
1. Your Next.js app measures things (heap, CPU, etc.)  → metrics.ts
2. Prometheus visits /api/metrics every 15s and stores numbers
3. Grafana asks Prometheus: 'what were the numbers over the last hour?'
4. Grafana draws the numbers as lines/graphs on this dashboard
METRICS AVAILABLE (all start with 'word_puzzle_' because of your prefix):
word_puzzle_nodejs_heap_size_used_bytes   → RAM your app is actively using
word_puzzle_nodejs_heap_size_total_bytes  → Total RAM allocated to the heap
word_puzzle_nodejs_external_memory_bytes  → Memory used outside the JS heap
word_puzzle_nodejs_gc_duration_seconds    → Time spent on garbage collection
word_puzzle_nodejs_eventloop_lag_seconds  → How delayed the event loop is
word_puzzle_process_cpu_seconds_total     → Total CPU time used
word_puzzle_process_open_fds              → Open file descriptors
__comment_uid: uid is a unique identifier for this dashboard. Must be unique across all dashboards.
title: Word Puzzle — App Metrics
__comment_tags: Tags are labels you can use to find/filter dashboards in Grafana.
__comment_timezone: timezone: 'browser' means use your browser's local timezone for time axes.
__comment_editable: editable: true lets you drag panels around and edit queries in the UI.
__comment_refresh: refresh: how often Grafana automatically re-fetches data from Prometheus. '30s' = every 30 seconds.
__comment_time: time: the default time window shown when you open the dashboard. 'now-1h' = last 1 hour.
__comment_panels_intro: Each object in this array is ONE chart/panel on the dashboard.
- type        → what kind of visual (timeseries = line chart, gauge, stat)
- title       → the heading shown above the chart
- gridPos     → where on the grid this panel sits (x, y, w=width, h=height)
- targets     → the PromQL queries that fetch data from Prometheus

panels.__comment2: type 'row' is just a collapsible section divider — not a real chart.
panels.title: Memory

__comment2: 'timeseries' is a LINE CHART over time.
This panel answers: 'How much JavaScript heap memory is my app using right now?'
PROMQL QUERY EXPLAINED:
word_puzzle_nodejs_heap_size_used_bytes
→ Simply read the current value of this metric.
No math needed — it's already in bytes.
panels.title: Heap Memory Used
panels.description: How many bytes of heap memory the Node.js process is actively using. A steadily growing line can indicate a memory leak.

targets.__comment: expr: the PromQL query sent to Prometheus
targets.__comment_legendFormat: legendFormat: label shown in the chart legend. {{instance}} inserts the scrape target address.

defaults.__comment_unit: unit: tells Grafana how to format the numbers on the Y axis. 'bytes' = auto-converts to KB/MB/GB.

panels.__comment: ─── PANEL 3: Heap Memory Total vs Used (comparison chart) ──────────────
__comment2: Two lines on one chart:
- Total heap allocated (reserved by Node.js runtime)
- Heap actually used by your code
The gap between them = free heap space. A shrinking gap is a warning sign.
panels.title: Heap: Total vs Used
panels.description: Total = memory Node.js reserved. Used = memory actually occupied by your objects. The gap between them is free space. Watch out if they converge.

__comment2: PROMQL QUERY EXPLAINED:
rate(word_puzzle_process_cpu_seconds_total[1m])
word_puzzle_process_cpu_seconds_total is a COUNTER — it only ever goes UP.
It counts the total CPU-seconds used since the process started.
rate(...[1m]) converts the raw counter into a RATE:
'How many CPU seconds were consumed per second, averaged over the last 1 minute?'
A value of 1.0 = 100% of one CPU core is used.
A value of 0.5 = 50% of one CPU core is used.
This is the most common way to show CPU usage in Prometheus.
panels.title: CPU Usage Rate
panels.description: CPU time consumed per second, averaged over the last 1 minute. 1.0 = one full CPU core. Values above 1 mean your app is using more than one core.

defaults.__comment_unit: percentunit: Grafana multiplies the value by 100 and shows a % symbol. 0.5 → 50%.

__comment2: Node.js is single-threaded. All your code runs on one 'event loop'.
If the loop is busy (e.g. processing a big computation), new requests
have to WAIT. That waiting time is the 'event loop lag'.
PROMQL QUERY:
word_puzzle_nodejs_eventloop_lag_seconds
→ Raw lag value in seconds.
A healthy app should have lag close to 0.
Spikes above 100ms (0.1s) are noticeable to users as slowness.
panels.title: Event Loop Lag
panels.description: How long new tasks must wait before the Node.js event loop runs them. High lag = your app is too busy and users notice slowness.

defaults.__comment_unit: s = seconds. Grafana will auto-format as ms/µs when values are small.

thresholds.__comment: Color the line green → yellow → red as lag increases

__comment2: WHAT IS GARBAGE COLLECTION (GC)?
Node.js automatically frees memory that's no longer needed.
This process is called 'garbage collection'. While GC runs, your
app is PAUSED — it can't process requests. Long GC pauses = bad UX.
PROMQL QUERY:
rate(word_puzzle_nodejs_gc_duration_seconds_sum[1m])
gc_duration_seconds_sum is a running total of time spent in GC.
rate(...[1m]) = seconds-per-second spent on GC over the last minute.
0.01 means 1% of the time is spent on GC — that's healthy.
0.1+ means 10%+ of time is GC — your app may have a memory problem.
panels.title: GC Duration Rate
panels.description: Fraction of time spent in garbage collection. High values mean your app has lots of short-lived objects being created and destroyed.

__comment2: WHAT ARE FILE DESCRIPTORS?
Every open file, socket, or network connection uses a 'file descriptor'.
Servers have a LIMIT on how many can be open at once (often 1024).
If you hit the limit, your app crashes with 'Too many open files'.
'stat' type shows a single big number — the current value.
It's good for values where you care about RIGHT NOW, not the history.
panels.title: Open File Descriptors
panels.description: Number of open files/sockets. If this climbs toward the OS limit – typically 1024 – your app may be leaking connections.

__comment2: External memory = memory allocated in C++ (outside the JS heap).
Mainly used by Buffers (e.g. when reading files or network data).
Normally low. A big spike here means lots of binary data in memory.
panels.title: External Memory (Buffers)
panels.description: Memory allocated outside the JavaScript heap — mainly Node.js Buffers used for I/O operations.

panels.__comment: ─── PANEL 11: Section heading — Quick At-a-glance stats ─────────────────
panels.title: At a Glance (current values)

__comment2: 'gauge' type draws a speedometer-style dial.
Great for showing a single current value with a visual range.
The max value is set to 512MB so the dial has a sensible scale.
panels.title: Heap Used Now

defaults.__comment_max: 536870912 = 512 MB in bytes. Adjust if your app uses more.

__comment2: PROMQL: up{job='word-puzzle-app'}
'up' is a special Prometheus metric. Prometheus sets it automatically:
1 = the target was reachable on the last scrape (app is UP)
0 = the target was not reachable (app is DOWN or scrape failed)
This gives you a simple green/red status indicator.
panels.title: App Status
panels.description: 1 = Prometheus can reach /api/metrics (app is up). 0 = scrape failed (app is down or unreachable).

mappings.__comment: Value mappings: show text instead of raw 0/1 numbers
